title: Reserves Adequacy & Import Cover

current.importCoverNote: Around 3 months of import cover by mid-2026; SBP reserves dipped in the latest week because of external debt repayments, while SBP indicated end-June inflows were expected to rebuild the buffer.

benchmark.label: IMF adequacy guideline
benchmark.note: The IMF and standard reserve-adequacy metrics use ~3 months of imports as a minimum comfort threshold for external resilience.

imfTarget.note: The IMF's end-FY25 SBP reserves target was $13.9bn; Pakistan exceeded it at $14.5bn. Reserves have since been rebuilt further.

drivers: IMF EFF + RSF disbursements (~$1.3bn in May 2026)
SBP-flagged end-June inflows of about $2.4bn after the June 19 repayment-driven reserves dip
Rollovers of bilateral deposits, notably from China and the Gulf
A contained current-account balance and stronger remittances
Tighter monetary policy supporting the rupee and inflows
context: Pakistan's reserves fell to barely two weeks of import cover in early 2023. Holding near 3 months by mid-2026 marks a major rebuild of the external buffer — though it remains thin by regional standards, volatile around debt-repayment weeks, and reliant on continued IMF and bilateral support.
methodologyNote: Import cover is foreign-exchange reserves divided by the average monthly import bill, expressed in months. Reserves figures are SBP-held (and total, including commercial banks) from SBP weekly data; import-cover months are as reported by SBP/IMF and reputable media and vary slightly by source and the import window used.
